title: Pods won't restart after reboot
body:
### Issue Description

pods can be created and restarted fine until reboot

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. `podman pod create test-pod`
2. `podman pod start test-pod`
3. `podman pod stop test-pod`
4. reboot
5. `podman restart test pod` 

failed


### Describe the results you received

```
Error: restarting container 98c10655c02562093a0ac011f7c1dfda09139c3f79ead83c5271c36e47ca893c: rootfs-overlay: creating overlay failed "/run/libpod/infra-container" from native overlay: mount overlay:/var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-containers/98c10655c02562093a0ac011f7c1dfda09139c3f79ead83c5271c36e47ca893c/rootfs/merge, flags: 0x4, data: lowerdir=/run/libpod/infra-container,upperdir=/var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-containers/98c10655c02562093a0ac011f7c1dfda09139c3f79ead83c5271c36e47ca893c/rootfs/upper,workdir=/var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-containers/98c10655c02562093a0ac011f7c1dfda09139c3f79ead83c5271c36e47ca893c/rootfs/work: stale file handle
```

### Describe the results you expected

pod should be restarted
(Or am i missing something?)

### podman info output

comments.body:
Doesn't reproduce over here.

Is your `/run` a tmpfs?
@mheon You are right, /run was in tmpfs. That's likely what I have missed. Worth to be mentioned in docs (especially since containers work fine). Since it works on persistent storage, should i close?
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Hitting this as well on Oracle Linux 10. /run is tmpfs. What is the solution?

You could try a `podman system check` or a manual removal of Podman's temporary files directory at `/run/libpod`. A proper cause and solution was never fully established.

Also, since I apparently missed this first time: `/run` is intended to be a tmpfs when using Podman. Temporary files stored there *must* be removed after reboot.

Thanks @mheon. The command `podman system check` does not return any output and removing `/run/libpod` did not solve the issue.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. `podman pod create test-pod`
2. `podman pod start test-pod`
3. `podman pod stop test-pod`
4. `reboot`
5. `podman pod start test-pod`

### Describe the results you received

```
Error: starting container 720d654ec5bd467f83d3b84be0a38bed99c118a66cd0ea2fddbe868b700d3c48: rootfs-overlay: creating overlay failed "/run/libpod/infra-container" from native overlay: mount overlay:/var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-containers/720d654ec5bd467f83d3b84be0a38bed99c118a66cd0ea2fddbe868b700d3c48/rootfs/merge, flags: 0x4, data: lowerdir=/run/libpod/infra-container,upperdir=/var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-containers/720d654ec5bd467f83d3b84be0a38bed99c118a66cd0ea2fddbe868b700d3c48/rootfs/upper,workdir=/var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-containers/720d654ec5bd467f83d3b84be0a38bed99c118a66cd0ea2fddbe868b700d3c48/rootfs/work,context="system_u:object_r:container_file_t:s0:c497,c1006": stale file handle
```

### Podman in a container
No

### Privileged Or Rootless
Privileged (root)

### Upstream Latest Release
Podman Version 5.6.0, Oracle Linux Server release 10.1

### Additional information
Removing the pod and recreating it solves the issue
@giuseppe Any ideas on this one?

the original reported issue seems to be solved (https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/27836#issuecomment-3703645338) and was caused by `/run` persisted over a reboot.

The new comment refers to an old Podman version.  Please try with the latest upstream release, or consider reaching out to the support for your enterprise distro if you can't upgrade to an upstream release.

Hi @giuseppe , I installed Podman version 5.8.2 from here (https://rpmfind.net/linux/RPM/centos-stream/10/appstream/aarch64/podman-5.8.2-1.el10.aarch64.html) and the issue persists with the same error:

```
Error: unable to start container "f2bb71793cf139fd01ae2770d67d1507aa801a3d2c04c037090aa6330659a708": rootfs-overlay: creating overlay failed "/run/libpod/infra-container" from native overlay: mount overlay:/var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-containers/f2bb71793cf139fd01ae2770d67d1507aa801a3d2c04c037090aa6330659a708/rootfs/merge, flags: 0x4, data: lowerdir=/run/libpod/infra-container,upperdir=/var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-containers/f2bb71793cf139fd01ae2770d67d1507aa801a3d2c04c037090aa6330659a708/rootfs/upper,workdir=/var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-containers/f2bb71793cf139fd01ae2770d67d1507aa801a3d2c04c037090aa6330659a708/rootfs/work,context="system_u:object_r:container_file_t:s0:c344,c660": stale file handle
```

Any ideas? This does not happen on Oracle Linux 9 with Podman 5.6.0

```
lowerdir=/run/libpod/infra-container,
upperdir=/var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-containers/f2bb71793cf139fd01ae2770d67d1507aa801a3d2c04c037090aa6330659a708/rootfs/upper,
workdir=/var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-containers/f2bb71793cf139fd01ae2770d67d1507aa801a3d2c04c037090aa6330659a708/rootfs/work
```

you are using an overlay volume on top of a transient mount (that is /run), so that the overlay index is not valid anymore.

Not sure what is the expectation here, if the volume is transient the overlay doesn't make sense.

If you want the lowerdir to be emptied and the container keeps running, then you can try with index=off, but even in this case overlay can misbehave.  The directory used for a lowerdir in overlay must exist as long as the container does

@giuseppe thanks for the explanation.

> Not sure what is the expectation here, if the volume is transient the overlay doesn't make sense.

If this setup does not make sense, then what is the reason the container is created with these settings?
My expectation is that the default behavior of podman would allow me to reboot the machine without causing the overlay invalidation.

To reiterate: the issue occurs after stopping the container and pod and rebooting the machine.
The configuration is correct if you don't reboot.  The issue is that you let the lowerdir disappear, so make sure it is on a file system that survives a reboot and that is never modified.
Any reason you can think of why this does not happen on Oracle Linux 9 with Podman 5.6.0? The /run filesystem is mounted as tmpfs there as well.

> Any reason you can think of why this does not happen on Oracle Linux 9 with Podman 5.6.0? The /run filesystem is mounted as tmpfs there as well.

The only reason I can think of is that the index= option is set to off for the overlay mount

So what would be a solution for this? Rebooting and starting pods worked fine before, at least it did a while ago, when I had podman 5.6 installed (I think).

Now every time I reboot the machine and try to start a pod I get something like this:
```
Error: starting container a1193b7766d0251b453a51b7a0faad5f1e74e956da8a5609088b460c48635520: rootfs-overlay: creating overlay failed "/run/libpod/infra-container" from native overlay: mount overlay:/mnt/raid/opt/containers/storage/overlay-containers/a1193b7766d0251b453a51b7a0faad5f1e74e956da8a5609088b460c48635520/rootfs/merge, flags: 0x4, data: lowerdir=/run/libpod/infra-container,upperdir=/mnt/raid/opt/containers/storage/overlay-containers/a1193b7766d0251b453a51b7a0faad5f1e74e956da8a5609088b460c48635520/rootfs/upper,workdir=/mnt/raid/opt/containers/storage/overlay-containers/a1193b7766d0251b453a51b7a0faad5f1e74e956da8a5609088b460c48635520/rootfs/work: stale file handle
Error: starting container 5847226b476df7e62432c86a15db6f20a2509520af5796b10bb1a0054dd5a856: a dependency of container 5847226b476df7e62432c86a15db6f20a2509520af5796b10bb1a0054dd5a856 failed to start: container state improper
```

The current kernel options for overlayfs in archlinux are:
```
# zgrep 'CONFIG_OVERLAY_FS' /proc/config.gz
CONFIG_OVERLAY_FS=m
CONFIG_OVERLAY_FS_REDIRECT_DIR=y
# CONFIG_OVERLAY_FS_REDIRECT_ALWAYS_FOLLOW is not set
CONFIG_OVERLAY_FS_INDEX=y
CONFIG_OVERLAY_FS_XINO_AUTO=y
CONFIG_OVERLAY_FS_METACOPY=y
# CONFIG_OVERLAY_FS_DEBUG is not set
```

### Minimal reproducible example on a *completely fresh* archlinux virtual machine with only podman installed:
```
# podman pod create --name python-test-pod
# podman create --name python-test --pod python-test-pod python:3-slim python -c 'print("Hello World")'
# podman pod start python-test-pod
# reboot
# podman pod start python-test-pod  # now this crashes
```

I also tried setting `mountopt = "nodev,index=off,metacopy=off"` in the `/etc/containers/storage.conf` file, without success.

If I recreate the pod and container(s) from zero, it starts up fine. For the time being I made a setup-pod.sh script that does this automatically. Quadlets also work, because they recreate the pod when started. But I would like to have this work just by itself.

Here is a trace log diff of `podman pod start python-test-pod` in the archlinux vm before and after rebooting:
(I trimmed the time= field from the beginning of the lines, so it is somewhat useful as a diff)

[python-test-pod.patch](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/28615856/python-test-pod.patch)

and the original files:

[test-before-reboot.err.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/28615680/test-before-reboot.err.log)

[test-after-reboot.err.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/28615859/test-after-reboot.err.log)

> I also tried setting `mountopt = "nodev,index=off,metacopy=off"` in the `/etc/containers/storage.conf` file, without success.
> 
> If I recreate the pod and container(s) from zero, it starts up fine. For the time being I made a setup-pod.sh script that does this automatically. Quadlets also work, because they recreate the pod when started. But I would like to have this work just by itself.

it is an invalid configuration.  The overlay mount must be stable across reboots, otherwise stuff like copy-up won't work.  I don't think there is anything to fix in Podman.

If you can't use a proper overlay, then do a copy first of the lower directory and use that as a regular volume without :O

I have done some more testing with the vm, and various permutations of the overlay options in the kernel.

TLDR: it is 100% the `CONFIG_OVERLAY_FS_INDEX=y` that makes pods crash.

I also would have expected `mountopt` in `/etc/containers/storage.conf` to have an effect on infra container overlays, but apparently it doesn't (because standalone containers work fine, so idk?)

I would appreciate any input on this, whether this is a kernel misconfiguration issue, or something wrong with podman itself.
And, if it is working as expected, I would like to see this mentioned somewhere in the documentation. (eg: something like "when using "overlay" storage driver, the CONFIG_OVERLAY_FS_INDEX kernel config must be unset or set to 'n'")

Here are the full test results and extra output diffs:
[test-overlayfs.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/28626272/test-overlayfs.zip)

(ah, you were faster to click the comment button than me 😂 )

> I also would have expected `mountopt` in `/etc/containers/storage.conf` to have an effect on infra container overlays, but apparently it doesn't (because standalone containers work fine, so idk?)
> 
> I would appreciate any input on this, whether this is a kernel misconfiguration issue, or something wrong with podman itself. And, if it is working as expected, I would like to see this mentioned somewhere in the documentation. (eg: something like "when using "overlay" storage driver, the CONFIG_OVERLAY_FS_INDEX kernel config must be unset or set to 'n'")

no, this is something wrong with the configuration.  Overlay requires the lower directories to be stable.  Even without CONFIG_OVERLAY_FS_INDEX, I think there are other cases where it might break, for example for copied-up files.

There is nothing we can do from Podman, except a check that the lower directories are on tmpfs, but this is not correct either because there are cases where the configuration is correct (that is when the pod/container doesn't survive the reboot).

> The overlay mount must be stable across reboots, otherwise stuff like copy-up won't work.

Also, if that's the case, then why is the infra container rootfs under /run/libpod/infra-container, which is (and should be) a tmpfs?

https://github.com/podman-container-tools/podman/issues/27836#issuecomment-4450180699

> You could try a `podman system check` or a manual removal of Podman's temporary files directory at `/run/libpod`. A proper cause and solution was never fully established.
> 
> Also, since I apparently missed this first time: `/run` is intended to be a tmpfs when using Podman. Temporary files stored there _must_ be removed after reboot.

Am I missing something? This doesn't make sense to me.

> There is nothing we can do from Podman, except a check that the lower directories are on tmpfs, but this is not correct either because there are cases where the configuration is correct (that is when the pod/container doesn't survive the reboot).

I see, alright.
